planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, strongly acidic, cool, deep, humus-rich, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade; mountain hemlock grows naturally at subalpine elevations (5,000–11,000 feet) along the Pacific Crest from Alaska to California, requiring cold winters and cool, moist summers. Does not tolerate heat, drought, or alkaline soils.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, strongly acidic, cool, deep, humus-rich, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade; mountain hemlock is the most cold-hardy and highest-elevation hemlock of the Pacific Coast — a distinctive subalpine conifer that grows from sea level in Alaska to treeline at 11,000 feet in the Sierra Nevada, forming the picturesque, wind-sculpted, krummholz (twisted) forms near treeline that are among the most iconic images of the high Sierra and Cascades. Unlike eastern hemlock, the needles of mountain hemlock radiate in all directions around the branchlet (like a bottlebrush) rather than lying flat in two rows — an immediately distinctive feature. The cones are proportionally larger than other hemlocks. Mountain hemlock forms pure open groves and mixes with whitebark pine, lodgepole pine, and subalpine fir at the highest tree-elevation zones. In gardens in cool, moist Pacific Northwest lowlands, it makes an outstanding ornamental specimen — more tolerant of cultivation than most subalpine conifers because the lowland maritime climate roughly mimics the cool, moist conditions it prefers.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral or slightly alkaline, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean to average soil in full sun; limber pine is highly drought tolerant once established and adapted to thin, rocky, nutrient-poor subalpine soils. Very long-lived — excellent for dry, high-elevation or exposed site plantings.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral or slightly alkaline, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean to average soil in full sun; limber pine is a classic five-needle (white pine group) pine of the Rocky Mountains and Great Basin, named for its extraordinarily flexible young branches and branchlets that can be tied in knots without breaking — a key adaptation to the heavy snow loads of its high-elevation, exposed, windswept native habitats on rocky ridges and subalpine parklands from Montana south to Arizona and New Mexico. Often grows alongside bristlecone pine and whitebark pine at the highest elevations, reaching 2,000–5,000 years of age in some ancient specimens. The large, wingless seeds (lacking the wind-dispersal wing of most pines) are dispersed almost exclusively by Clark's nutcracker, which caches thousands of seeds annually. Like whitebark pine, it is threatened by white pine blister rust (Cronartium ribicola). An excellent cold-hardy, drought-tolerant ornamental pine for high plains, foothills, and rocky mountain sites.

planting.note: Plant seeds (stratified) or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky, lean to average soil in full sun; whitebark pine is a keystone subalpine species growing at treeline and requiring cold winters, cool summers, and excellent drainage. Threatened by blister rust, mountain pine beetle, and climate change. Plant only rust-resistant stock from reputable sources.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring (use only rust-resistant certified stock) in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky, lean to average soil in full sun; whitebark pine is a keystone subalpine species of the highest Rocky Mountain treeline zones — one of the most ecologically critical trees in the western mountain ecosystem, providing essential high-calorie seeds for grizzly bears (up to 50% of pre-denning grizzly diet in some years), black bears, Clark's nutcrackers, and many other wildlife species. The tree has no seed wing — its seeds cannot be wind-dispersed; instead Clark's nutcracker (Nucifraga columbiana) is the primary and essentially only effective seed disperser, harvesting thousands of seeds per bird per year and caching them in groups that, when uncollected, establish new trees. Severely threatened — white pine blister rust, mountain pine beetle, and climate change have killed the majority of whitebark pines in many areas. Listed as threatened under the Endangered Species Act in 2022. Ancient trees at treeline grow as wind-sculpted krummholz mats no taller than the winter snow. One of the most ecologically important trees in the western US.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, deep, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; ponderosa pine is highly drought tolerant once established (after 2–3 growing seasons) but requires regular watering during establishment. Tap-rooted — does not tolerate waterlogging or compacted soils. Grows extremely fast in youth with good water.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, deep, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; ponderosa pine is the most widely distributed pine in North America — covering more land area than any other pine and forming vast, open, parklike forests across the entire western interior from British Columbia to Mexico and from the Pacific Coast ranges to the Great Plains. The mature bark is one of the most distinctive of any tree — developing large, interlocking, jigsaw-puzzle-like plates of orange-cinnamon to yellow-orange bark that, on a warm day, exude a remarkable, unmistakable vanilla and butterscotch fragrance from the bark's resin pockets. The bark fragrance is one of nature's most memorable and beloved scents, often serving as the first signal to hikers that they have entered ponderosa pine forest. The open, parklike ponderosa forests maintained by frequent low-intensity fire have been severely disrupted by a century of fire suppression, creating dangerously overcrowded forests prone to catastrophic crown fire. Among the most important forest restoration and thinning challenges in the western US. Makes an excellent large landscape pine for dry, western gardens.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, average to lean, sandy or loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; lodgepole pine is highly adaptable, tolerating boggy, acidic soils (coastal form) through well-drained mountain soils. The inland form (var. latifolia) is used for large landscape and windbreak plantings; the coastal form (var. contorta) is more compact.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, average to lean, sandy or loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; lodgepole pine is one of the most ecologically important and ecologically fascinating conifers in western North America — growing from bog edges at sea level on the Pacific Coast (shore pine, var. contorta) through the montane forests of the Cascades and Sierra Nevada (var. murrayana) to the vast subalpine lodgepole forests of the Rocky Mountains (var. latifolia). Named for its traditional use by Native peoples as the straight, slender poles for constructing tipis and lodges. The Rocky Mountain lodgepole is famous for its serotinous cones — cones sealed with resin that remain on the tree for years, opening only in the intense heat of a forest fire, releasing a massive seed rain to regenerate burned areas. This fire-adaptation allows lodgepole to be the dominant post-fire forest species across millions of acres of the Rockies. Severely impacted by mountain pine beetle (Dendroctonus ponderosae) outbreaks, which have killed over 70,000 square miles of lodgepole forest in western Canada and the US since 2000.
